subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Inclusion of two accurate, clearly labelled AD/AS diagrams with all essential elements (axes, curves, equilibrium points, gaps).
Full and precise explanation of diagram mechanics linking curve shifts to price level and real GDP changes.
Effective reference to each diagram in the text (âDiagram 1â and âDiagram 2â), showing integration of visuals and commentary.
weaknesses: Diagram 2 omits a dashed LRAS1âLRAS2 shift to illustrate long-run supply effects.
Minor suggestion: annotate quantitative magnitudes (e.g., shift sizes) to enrich the visual impact.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both diagram strands meet the highest descriptors: diagrams are relevant, accurate, correctly labelled and fully explained. The only shortcoming is the missing long-run supply shift in Diagram 2, but this does not detract from an otherwise flawless depiction and explanation.

strengths: Consistent, appropriate use of macroeconomic terms (e.g., aggregate demand, recessionary gap, multiplier effect).
Terminology is integrated into analysis rather than listed, supporting clear economic argumentation.
weaknesses: No significant lapses, but the commentary could briefly define one or two less familiar terms to aid reader comprehension.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses relevant terminology accurately and consistently throughout, fully meeting the top band descriptor for appropriate sustained usage.

strengths: Sound application of monetary and fiscal theory in explaining AD shifts and LRAS changes.
Clear discussion of the multiplier effect and step-by-step RRR mechanics, showing solid theoretical grounding.
weaknesses: Analysis remains surface-level: lacks discussion of curve slopes, elasticities and quantitative magnitudes.
Transmission mechanisms (money-creation process, bond-financing dynamics) and alternative scenarios are not fully developed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The commentary applies relevant theory appropriately throughout and provides logical analysis, but it stops short of the depth and development (elasticities, magnitudes, counter-scenarios) required for the highest band.

strengths: Key concept âChangeâ is explicitly declared and tied to policy shifts in the header and opening.
The concept is appropriately chosen and linked to monetary and fiscal interventions.
weaknesses: The linkage of âChangeâ is not sustained uniformly through all evaluative sections.
Could more deeply integrate the concept by reflecting on systemic change in each policy analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Identification meets the top band, but connection to the article is partial rather than fully woven through the commentary, meriting the second band.

strengths: Judgments are balanced, considering pros and cons for consumers, firms and government.
Supporting reasoning effectively links judgments to theory (e.g., multiplier, crowding-out).
Conclusion succinctly summarizes trade-offs with clear evaluative balance.
weaknesses: Evaluation lacks concrete policy prioritization or clear recommendations.
Reasoning is sound but sometimes generalized; more nuanced evidence (data, time lags) would strengthen arguments.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student makes effective, balanced judgments with logical reasoning, but falls short of exemplary evaluative depth and policy recommendation required for the top band.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student analyses short-run impacts (higher real GDP, inflation risk) and long-run implications (potential LRAS shift, municipal-debt constraint). The analysis is logical and generally accurate but remains surface-levelâquantitative magnitudes, elasticity considerations and alternative scenarios are absentâso it is âappropriate but could be more developed.â

The student includes two macro-level AD/AS diagrams, each with clearly labelled axes (Price Level Â¥ and Real GDP), equilibrium points, curves (AD1, AD2, SRAS, LRAS) and directional arrows. The diagrams are free of technical errors and accurately depict the shifts described. All essential elements (original and new equilibria, recessionary gap, potential output) are marked, fulfilling the rubric requirement for relevant, accurate and correctly labelled diagrams.

The student opens by defining change as a shift in government intervention and periodically links this to the Politburoâs adoption of new monetary and fiscal tools. However, the discussion does not weave the concept consistently through all evaluative sections, so the linkage, while clear, is not fully integrated, meriting a partial (band 2) explanation.

Relevant theoryâexpansionary monetary policy, expansionary fiscal policy, short-run versus long-run AD/AS impacts and LRAS shiftsâis applied to every section of the article. While the treatment covers both demand- and supply-side channels, it lacks depth on transmission mechanisms (e.g., money-creation process, bond-financing dynamics), so the application is sound but not "thorough."

Reasoning links each judgment to theoretical channels (confidence dampening usefulness, multiplier effects, crowding-out). The logic is sound but sometimes generalized, lacking nuanced evidence (e.g., data magnitudes, time lags). Hence, the supporting reasoning is effective but not fully developed.

The key concept "Change" is explicitly declared in the header and directly tied to policy shifts. The concept choice is appropriate and not reused elsewhere, meeting the rubricâs highest expectation for identification.

The commentary explicitly refers to âDiagram 1â and âDiagram 2,â explaining how lowering the RRR shifts AD rightward and how fiscal stimulus plus supply-side effects shift AD and LRAS. The explanations link the plotted movements to price level and real GDP changes and relate them back to Chinaâs 5 % growth target and potential inflation pressures. This constitutes a full and accurate explanation of each diagramâs mechanics and relevance.

Throughout the piece the student consistently employs macroeconomic terms such as âaggregate demand,â ârecessionary gap,â âreserve requirement ratio,â âmultiplier effect,â âdemand-pull inflation,â and âcrowding-out.â Usage is contextually correct and integrated into the argument, satisfying the descriptor for appropriate terminology used throughout.

Judgments are present on effectiveness, inflation risk, and long-run debt sustainability; pros and cons for consumers, firms and government are acknowledged, showing balance. Yet recommendations or prioritisation are limited, so judgments are effective but not exemplary.

annotations.comment: The metadata table effectively declares the key concept âChange,â providing clarity on the studentâs focus. This explicit identification supports the rubricâs highest level for linking concept to the commentary.
Discuss potential crowding-out effects by noting how increased government borrowing could raise interest rates, $$i \u2191,$$ to support your evaluation of fiscal limits.
To deepen the conceptual frame, include a brief rationale for selecting âChangeââfor example, linking the dynamic policy shifts directly to the conceptâs definition in the introduction.
The article section relies heavily on direct quotations rather than concise paraphrasing; summarizing the Politburoâs main points more succinctly would free space for deeper analysis.
Consider cutting down the lengthy policy description in this paragraph to focus on analyzing implications rather than recounting Reutersâ narrative.
Integrate the key concept âChangeâ explicitly within this summary by reflecting on how each policy adjustment exemplifies systemic change.
The introductory paragraph defines AD and AS clearly, using precise terminology. This sets a strong foundation for subsequent analysis.
To reinforce the AD discussion, include the equation $$AD=C+I+G+(X-M)$$ when defining aggregate demand components.
The step-by-step explanation of how RRR cuts increase money supply and shift AD is clear and logically structured, demonstrating sound application of monetary theory.
This discussion lacks any reference to the actual magnitude of recent RRR cuts; citing the specific basis-point change would strengthen the analysis.
Consider introducing the money-multiplier formula, $$ rac{1}{RRR + CR + ER},$$ to illustrate quantitatively how reserve requirement changes amplify money supply.
Diagram 1 is accurately drawn, correctly labelled, and the recessionary gap is clear. This meets the criteria for high-quality diagrams.
The explanation of demand-side factors does not discuss the slopes of the AD and SRAS curves; incorporating elasticity would deepen the analysis of price impacts.
It would be beneficial to mention potential SRAS curve shifts due to cost changes, strengthening the interplay between AD and AS.
Diagram 2 is clearly presented with both AD shifts and narrative support for fiscal expansion, fulfilling the diagram accuracy and explanation criteria.
The figure for fiscal policy omits any depiction of the LRAS shift; adding a dashed LRAS1âLRAS2 would illustrate the long-run supply impact.
The discussion of the multiplier effect captures the circular flow and job creation effectively, showing good theoretical application.
While the multiplier is noted, providing a numerical estimate or range (e.g., 1.5â2.5) would illustrate its real-world magnitude more convincingly.
The long-run shift of LRAS due to technology investments is well explained, highlighting supply-side growth drivers.
The supply-side section could discuss specific productivity enhancementsâsuch as human capital improvementsâto illustrate how LRAS shifts occur.
The stakeholder analysis balances consumer, business, and government perspectives effectively, showing clear evaluative judgment.
To enhance evaluation, the student could recommend which policy mix (monetary vs. fiscal vs. supply-side) should be prioritized based on Chinaâs debt constraints.
The conclusion succinctly summarizes trade-offs and reinforces the main evaluation points with balanced judgment.
The closing paragraph could be strengthened by offering a clear policy recommendation, prioritizing specific reforms to avoid generic advice.
The works cited entry follows IB conventions and accurately references the Reuters article, demonstrating good academic practice.
For consistency, integrate in-text citations (e.g., â(Reuters, 2024)â) within your commentary when directly referencing article content.
